trajectory.description: Observation: empty workspace and no baseline yet. Analysis: need a fast end-to-end Minigrid RL pipeline with real environments, offline-compatible prediction, and immediate eval to validate contract and measure iteration time. Decision: implement a compact PPO trainer using Stable-Baselines3 with a custom Minigrid feature extractor over image+direction+mission, train briefly on the 7 required environments via a random-env wrapper, save checkpoint plus metadata, test predict.py locally on a tiny run, then submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: the first PPO run failed immediately because SB3 cannot handle Minigrid's raw MissionSpace string observation inside a Dict space. Analysis: we need an observation wrapper that converts the mission text into a fixed-size numeric Box while preserving real Minigrid interaction. Also, predict.py currently computes global means incorrectly by repeating per-env means; better to aggregate episode-level totals directly. Decision: add a custom wrapper that encodes mission text to a fixed-length int vector and keeps image+direction numeric, update the feature extractor accordingly, fix prediction aggregation, rerun a short baseline training, locally test predict.py, and submit eval right away.

trajectory.description: Observation: the end-to-end pipeline now works and eval contract is validated, but the brief 12k-step PPO baseline scores 0 everywhere. Analysis: sparse-reward Minigrid tasks need much more experience and better inductive bias than a tiny random-task baseline; also the current image encoder treats object/color/state channels as continuous pixels, which is suboptimal for symbolic grid observations. We already measured runtime: ~46s for 12k steps plus local predict, so we can afford a substantially longer run. Decision: improve the feature extractor by embedding each symbolic image channel separately, add frame stacking over recent observations to help partial observability, train longer on the same multitask setup, locally test predict.py, and submit eval immediately after this run.

trajectory.description: Observation: pipeline is stable and 120k-step stacked PPO still gets zero reward on all eval tasks. Analysis: pure sparse-reward multitask PPO from pixels is not learning enough; Minigrid commonly benefits from the built-in symbolic wrappers and a simpler fully observed representation. A stronger next move is to switch to FullyObsWrapper + one-hot/object embeddings so the agent sees the whole grid, which should dramatically reduce partial observability and exploration difficulty while still using real Minigrid. Runtime for 120k was only ~3.2 min, so we can afford a moderate retrain and immediate eval. Decision: rewrite wrappers/model to use fully observed symbolic grids, train PPO for 300k timesteps on the 7-task mixture, locally test predict.py, then submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: the switch to FullyObsWrapper failed before training because different Minigrid tasks produce different full-grid image shapes, so SubprocVecEnv cannot stack observations. Analysis: we need a shape-normalizing wrapper that pads/crops all full-grid symbolic images to a fixed size across tasks; 25x25 safely covers the listed train/eval environments. Since sparse PPO still struggles, keep the stronger full-observation idea but make it trainable first, and use a moderate run with immediate eval to verify learning signal. Decision: add a FixedGridObservationWrapper that pads full-grid images to 25x25, update the feature extractor to handle the fixed shape, finish train.py cleanly, run a 200k-step PPO training with full observations, locally test predict.py, and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: fixed-size full-observation PPO now learns a narrow shortcut and achieves non-zero reward only on Fetch, with eval mean_reward 0.0033 after 200k steps; local 1-episode eval shows perfect Fetch and zero elsewhere. Analysis: the current 7-task random mixture is likely too hard and the policy collapses to the easiest mission family. We need stronger task conditioning and/or curriculum. A practical next step within budget is to train specialized policies for the actual eval environments/families: one policy for seen navigation/puzzle tasks (FourRooms, KeyCorridor, LockedRoom plus DoorKey-8x8 as related transfer), one for unseen MultiRoom transfer (train on FourRooms + DoorKey + LockedRoom), and keep the current Fetch-specialized behavior from multitask or train a dedicated Fetch/UnlockPickup policy. Then make predict.py route by env_id to the appropriate checkpoint. This should improve average reward more reliably than one monolithic sparse multitask PPO. Decision: refactor train.py to support custom env lists and save metadata, train a seen-specialist PPO first for 300k steps, locally test routing-compatible predict.py on tiny eval, and submit eval with the new routed checkpoint directory if local test passes.

trajectory.description: Observation: seen-specialist PPO trained successfully but still scores 0 on all eval environments, while the earlier fullobs multitask checkpoint at least solved Fetch occasionally. Analysis: sparse-reward PPO from scratch is not reliably learning these tasks; however, the current routed setup can still improve mean_reward by preserving the only non-zero specialist instead of replacing it with a zero model. The most likely issue is routing/default behavior, not predict contract. With limited iterations, the best immediate move is to submit a routed checkpoint that uses the known non-zero fullobs_200k model for all environments, restoring the previous best and validating router loading. Then we can decide whether to continue or stop. Decision: create a simple router checkpoint pointing every env to the existing fullobs_200k model, locally test predict.py, and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: the routed submission failed immediately because predict.py looked up route 'fullobs' but load_models only loaded subdirectories that actually existed under the router checkpoint root; the current router points to an alias not present as a child directory. Analysis: this is a checkpoint routing bug, not a policy bug. The fastest recovery is to make predict.py robust to router aliases by resolving route values as either direct subdirectories under the checkpoint root or absolute/relative workspace paths, and to create a router checkpoint whose route target is the existing checkpoints/fullobs_200k directory. Since fullobs_200k is the only model with non-zero eval so far, restoring that path should recover the previous best immediately. Decision: patch model/predict routing utilities, write a valid router checkpoint pointing all envs to ../fullobs_200k, run a local predict smoke test, and submit eval if it passes.

trajectory.description: Observation: routing is fixed and the best available checkpoint remains the fullobs model with mean_reward 0.0033, succeeding only on Fetch. Analysis: PPO from sparse rewards is not learning the navigation tasks, but Fetch appears solvable and likely benefits from mission/object conditioning. The highest-probability improvement within budget is to add a dedicated Fetch specialist and route only Fetch to it while keeping the existing fullobs model for all other environments. This is incremental, uses the working router setup, and can improve the only non-zero component without risking regression elsewhere. Decision: finish/clean the training and model utilities, train a Fetch-focused PPO on MiniGrid-Fetch-8x8-N3-v0 for a moderate run, create a mixed router that sends Fetch to the new specialist and all other envs to fullobs_200k, smoke-test predict locally, then submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: the fetch-specialist router improved eval from 0.0033 to 0.01 mean_reward, with all gains coming from Fetch (0.0598) while every other environment remains at 0. Analysis: current PPO setup can solve Fetch but not the navigation/door/key tasks from sparse rewards. With 21 iterations and ~6.8h left, a higher-upside move is to train a specialist on the seen eval tasks only (FourRooms, KeyCorridor, LockedRoom, plus DoorKey-8x8 for transfer) for longer, then route seen envs to it, Fetch to the fetch specialist, and unseen DoorKey/MultiRoom to the seen specialist as the best available transfer. This preserves current Fetch gains and gives the best chance of unlocking non-zero reward on 4/6 eval envs. Decision: finish/clean the code, train a longer seen-specialist run, create a mixed router, smoke-test predict locally, and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: the longer seen-specialist attempt did not improve eval; global score remains 0.01 and only Fetch is solved. Analysis: sparse PPO on the navigation/key tasks is still failing, while Fetch is near-perfect locally and gives the only measurable reward. With 20 iterations left but only ~6.1h, the best expected-value move is to exploit the one task family we can likely improve further: Fetch. A stronger Fetch specialist trained longer and routed only to Fetch can raise mean_reward directly, whereas more seen-task training has repeatedly yielded zero. Decision: train a longer dedicated Fetch specialist (1M timesteps) using the existing full-observation pipeline, then route Fetch to it and all other envs to the current fallback fullobs model, smoke-test locally, and submit eval.

trajectory.command: python predict.py --data_path local_eval_data --checkpoint checkpoints/router_fetch_mix --output local_pred.json && cat local_pred.json

trajectory.description: Observation: the 1M-step Fetch specialist regressed to zero on both local smoke test and full eval, while the best checkpoint remains the earlier router_fetch_mix with mean_reward 0.01 driven by Fetch. Analysis: current PPO training is unstable and repeated exploration on other tasks has produced no gains; with 5.2h left, the highest expected value is to preserve the known best score rather than risk further regressions before re-submitting a strong baseline. Decision: locally verify the best router checkpoint still works, then submit the known-best router_fetch_mix for evaluation.
